segments.conditions: Pacific Mail Steamship Company and Oregon Steamship Company ran regular service from San Francisco's Embarcadero, the wharves crowded with freight wagons and stevedores loading lumber south and manufactured goods north. The route hugged the coast past Cape Mendocino — notoriously rough water where the Pacific swells hit the continental shelf and passengers who boarded cheerfully in the calm of the Bay spent hours gripping the rail. The final hazard was the Columbia River bar, one of the most dangerous river entrances in the world, where ocean swells collide with the outgoing current and ships sometimes waited days for a bar pilot to guide them across. Chinese passengers were confined to steerage class below the waterline, sharing space with cargo.

The extended route continued from Portland through the Columbia River and into Puget Sound, threading between forested islands where sawmills sent columns of smoke above the tree line. By the 1880s regular service connected all major West Coast ports, with ships stopping at Astoria, Eureka, and smaller lumber and fishing towns along the way. Passengers bound for Seattle's growing Chinatown rode the same ships that carried Douglas fir timbers and canned salmon south to San Francisco markets.

segments.to: Victoria, British Columbia / Vancouver, British Columbia
segments.conditions: This route was critical for Chinese immigrants because Canadian ports sometimes offered entry when American ports were closed by exclusion law. Ships crossed into British waters through the Strait of Juan de Fuca, arriving at Victoria's Outer Wharf where immigration inspectors applied different rules than their American counterparts. The 'paper son' networks — families who created documentation claiming new arrivals as sons of established residents — used this route to move between jurisdictions. A man turned away at Angel Island might board a coastal steamer and try again at Victoria.

who_used_it: Chinese immigrants redistributing from San Francisco to Portland, Seattle, and smaller West Coast communities. Gold Rush miners heading north to Oregon and Washington. Lumber workers following the timber industry up the coast. After the Chinese Exclusion Act of 1882, the coastal steamer was how Chinese workers already in the United States moved between Chinatowns — Angel Island processed new arrivals, but the coastal steamer connected the established communities in San Francisco, Portland, Seattle, and Victoria, carrying workers to wherever the labor networks found openings in canneries, laundries, and railroad camps.
narrative_hook: In {year}, {ancestor_name} likely traveled the Pacific coastal route from San Francisco to {destination} — a two-day voyage along a coastline of fog, redwood forests, and river bars so dangerous that ships sometimes waited days for safe passage across the Columbia.
source_references: John Haskell Kemble, 'San Francisco Bay: A Pictorial Maritime History' (Cornell Maritime Press, 1957)
Gordon Newell, 'Ships of the Inland Sea: The Story of the Puget Sound Steamboats' (Binfords & Mort, 1951)
Carlos Arnaldo Schwantes, 'The Pacific Northwest: An Interpretive History' (University of Nebraska Press, 1996)
Chris Friday, 'Organizing Asian American Labor: The Pacific Coast Canned-Salmon Industry, 1870-1942' (Temple University Press, 1994)
Robert J. Schwendinger, 'Ocean of Bitter Dreams: Maritime Relations Between China and the United States, 1850-1915' (Great Basin Press, 1988)
